title: The Upload That Worked Everywhere Else

narrator.personality: Patient. Has seen this before. Mentions that the developer checked IAM policies for two hours before looking at the browser console. States this without judgment.

story_beats.message: The frontend developer just sent another message in Slack. She says the presigned URL is valid because she tested it with curl. She attached the curl output. She is now re-reading the IAM policy JSON for the third time.

A backend engineer joined the thread. He says the API Gateway logs show the presigned URL was generated correctly. He also checked the S3 bucket policy. Everything looks fine from the server side. He asked if anyone has checked the browser console.
The IAM policy and presigned URL are both correct. The upload works from non-browser clients. The difference is in how the browser makes the request, not in the permissions.

glossary.CORS: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing. A browser security feature that blocks web pages from making requests to a different domain unless the server explicitly allows it.
glossary.preflight request: An automatic OPTIONS request the browser sends before certain cross-origin requests (like PUT) to ask the server if the real request is allowed.
glossary.presigned URL: A temporary URL that includes authentication information in its query string, allowing anyone with the URL to perform a specific S3 operation without AWS credentials.
glossary.origin: A combination of protocol, domain, and port (e.g., https://app.pollen.io). Two URLs with different origins are considered cross-origin by the browser.
glossary.same-origin policy: A browser security rule that prevents JavaScript on one origin from reading responses from a different origin, unless the server opts in via CORS headers.
glossary.origin request policy: A CloudFront configuration that controls which headers, cookies, and query strings CloudFront forwards from the viewer to the origin server.
glossary.cache invalidation: A CloudFront operation that removes cached objects from edge locations before they expire, forcing CloudFront to fetch fresh copies from the origin.
glossary.OPTIONS method: An HTTP method used by browsers for preflight requests. It asks the server what methods and headers are allowed for cross-origin requests.

narrative_arc.call: A browser upload fails silently. The same presigned URL works from curl, Postman, and a Python script. The developer begins reading IAM policies.
narrative_arc.threshold: Two hours pass. The developer has read the IAM policy three times. A backend engineer joins and asks whether anyone has checked the browser console.
narrative_arc.trials: The browser console shows a CORS error. The S3 bucket has no CORS configuration. After adding CORS, the upload still fails because CloudFront strips the Origin header before it reaches S3.
narrative_arc.revelation: The problem has two layers. S3 needs CORS rules. CloudFront needs an origin request policy that forwards the Origin, Access-Control-Request-Headers, and Access-Control-Request-Method headers. Both must be fixed together.
narrative_arc.return: The upload works. The developer adds a note to the team wiki: if it works in curl but not the browser, check CORS first.

components.name: Browser at app.pollen.io
components.role: Sends preflight OPTIONS requests and enforces same-origin policy before cross-origin PUT uploads
components.failure_impact: If the browser receives no CORS headers, it blocks the upload silently with no user-facing error message

components.name: API Gateway at api.pollen.io
components.role: Generates presigned URLs via Lambda for browser-based document uploads to S3
components.failure_impact: If the API Gateway fails, the browser cannot obtain a presigned URL to upload documents

components.name: CloudFront distribution d-EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE
components.role: Caches responses and forwards requests to S3, but strips Origin header without a CORS origin request policy
components.failure_impact: If CloudFront strips CORS headers, S3 cannot respond to preflight requests and browser uploads fail

components.name: S3 bucket pollen-docs-prod
components.role: Stores uploaded documents and receives PUT requests via presigned URLs
components.failure_impact: Without CORS configuration, S3 returns 403 on OPTIONS preflight requests from browsers

system_narration.data_flow: Browser requests a presigned URL from API Gateway. Lambda generates the URL and returns it. Browser uses the URL to PUT a file to S3 through CloudFront. Before the PUT, the browser sends an OPTIONS preflight to CloudFront. CloudFront forwards the request to S3 but strips the Origin header. S3 has no CORS configuration and returns 403 on the OPTIONS. The browser blocks the PUT.
system_narration.what_broke: Two things broke. First, the S3 bucket had no CORS configuration, so it could not respond to preflight requests with the required Access-Control-Allow-Origin header. Second, CloudFront used the default CachingOptimized policy with no origin request policy, so it did not forward the Origin, Access-Control-Request-Headers, or Access-Control-Request-Method headers to S3. Even after fixing S3, CloudFront would cache non-CORS responses and serve them to browsers.

hints.text: The upload works outside the browser. What is different about browser HTTP requests?
Check the browser developer tools Network tab. Look at the request headers.
Browsers send preflight OPTIONS requests for cross-origin PUT requests.
The S3 bucket has no CORS configuration. Run `aws s3api get-bucket-cors`.
CloudFront is also stripping the Origin header. Check the cache behavior's origin request policy.

resolution.root_cause: The S3 bucket pollen-docs-prod has no CORS configuration, causing browsers to block cross-origin PUT requests via presigned URLs. Additionally, the CloudFront distribution d-EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE is configured with a cache policy that does not forward the Origin, Access-Control-Request-Headers, or Access-Control-Request-Method headers to S3, so even after adding CORS config to S3, CloudFront would strip the headers needed for CORS negotiation.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies that the S3 bucket has no CORS configuration, causing browser uploads to fail
Player identifies that CloudFront is not forwarding the Origin header to S3
Player proposes a correct CORS JSON configuration for the S3 bucket that allows PUT from the application origin
Player explains why curl and Postman work but browsers do not -- browsers enforce the same-origin policy and send preflight OPTIONS requests

learning_objectives: CORS is enforced by browsers, not servers -- non-browser clients like curl and Postman skip CORS entirely
S3 buckets require explicit CORS configuration to respond to cross-origin requests with the correct Access-Control headers
CloudFront must forward Origin, Access-Control-Request-Headers, and Access-Control-Request-Method headers for CORS to work end-to-end
Presigned URLs handle authentication and authorization but do not bypass CORS -- these are separate mechanisms
sop_steps: Confirm the symptom: browser upload fails, non-browser clients (curl, Postman, SDK) succeed with the same presigned URL.
Open browser developer tools. Check the Console and Network tabs for CORS errors and a failed OPTIONS preflight request.
Run `aws s3api get-bucket-cors --bucket pollen-docs-prod` to check whether CORS is configured on the S3 bucket.

If CORSRules is empty or missing, create a CORS configuration JSON allowing the application origin (https://app.pollen.io), the required methods (GET, PUT, POST, HEAD), AllowedHeaders [*], and ExposeHeaders [ETag, x-amz-request-id].
Apply the CORS configuration: `aws s3api put-bucket-cors --bucket pollen-docs-prod --cors-configuration file://cors.json`.
Test the browser upload. If it still fails, check CloudFront.

Inspect the CloudFront distribution's default cache behavior. Check whether an origin request policy is attached and whether it forwards Origin, Access-Control-Request-Headers, and Access-Control-Request-Method.
If no origin request policy is set, attach the AWS-managed CORS-S3Origin policy (ID: 88a5eaf4-2fd4-4709-b370-b4c650ea3fcf) to the cache behavior.
Create a CloudFront cache invalidation for /* to clear stale cached responses that lack CORS headers: `aws cloudfront create-invalidation --distribution-id EDFDVBD6EXAMPLE --paths "/*"`.
Verify the fix: upload a file from the browser at https://app.pollen.io. Confirm no CORS errors in the console and the file appears in the S3 bucket.
Add a checklist item to the team's S3 bucket creation runbook: if browser clients will access the bucket directly, configure CORS and the CloudFront origin request policy before the feature ships.

related_failure_modes.scenario: CORS configuration exists but AllowedOrigins does not include the application domain
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: S3 returns a 403 with the message 'This CORS request is not allowed' instead of 'CORS is not enabled for this bucket'. The fix is updating AllowedOrigins rather than adding the entire CORS configuration.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Use explicit origin values instead of wildcards in production. Review CORS AllowedOrigins when adding new frontend domains.

related_failure_modes.scenario: CloudFront caches a non-CORS response and serves it to browser clients
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: CORS is configured correctly on S3, and direct requests to S3 succeed from the browser. But requests through CloudFront fail intermittently because CloudFront cached a response from a non-browser client that lacked CORS headers.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Always attach an origin request policy that forwards Origin and CORS headers when S3 is the origin. Vary the cache on the Origin header.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Presigned URL signature mismatch due to extra headers
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: The error is SignatureDoesNotMatch, not a CORS error. This happens when the browser adds headers (like Content-Type with a different value) that were not included when the presigned URL was generated.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Include all headers the browser will send (especially Content-Type) in the SignedHeaders when generating the presigned URL.

related_failure_modes.scenario: S3 bucket policy denies the OPTIONS method
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: CORS is configured on the bucket, but a restrictive bucket policy explicitly denies all methods except GET and PUT. The OPTIONS preflight is denied by the bucket policy before S3 evaluates the CORS configuration.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Ensure bucket policies do not deny the OPTIONS method when CORS is required. S3 needs to receive the OPTIONS request to return CORS headers.

sop_practices: When enabling direct browser uploads to S3, always configure CORS on the bucket and verify it with a preflight test using curl -X OPTIONS before deploying to production.

When placing CloudFront in front of S3 and expecting browser requests, attach the managed CORS-S3Origin origin request policy (ID: 88a5eaf4-2fd4-4709-b370-b4c650ea3fcf) to forward Origin, Access-Control-Request-Headers, and Access-Control-Request-Method.
After any CORS or CloudFront policy change, create a cache invalidation for /* to ensure stale responses without CORS headers are cleared from edge locations.
Add CORS verification to the team's deployment checklist for any feature that involves browser-to-S3 direct access, including presigned URL uploads and static asset hosting.
